behaviors.description: Invalid signature request is rejected with HTTP 401 and the body is not forwarded to the queue.
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is deterministic HMAC-SHA256 cryptography plus a conditional branch. A unit test calling the handler with a mismatched signature can verify both the 401 response and that the mocked queue's enqueue() method is never called. No real infrastructure (network, subprocess, persistent store) is required to catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: POST a webhook request with an X-Signature-256 header that does not match the computed HMAC-SHA256 digest of the body. Verify: (1) HTTP response is 401 Unauthorized, (2) the mock queue's enqueue() method was not called, (3) no side effects occur.

behaviors.description: An event is durably written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: The failure being prevented is a race condition where the HTTP response is sent before the queue write operation actually completes. A unit test with an instant-returning mock queue would always pass, regardless of whether the code properly awaits the enqueue operation. Real queue latency (or a realistic simulation with instrumentation) is required to reveal if the code structure truly guarantees persistence before response. A mock could hide: (1) missing await on enqueue, (2) improper error handling of queue write, (3) timing issues with the real queue's fsync or persistence.
behaviors.test_description: POST a valid webhook request. Instrument the persistent queue (or its client) to record the exact timestamp when enqueue() completes. Record the exact timestamp when the HTTP response is sent to the client. Verify: enqueue() completion timestamp ≤ response send timestamp. Run multiple times with realistic queue latency (e.g., 10-100ms) to ensure ordering is consistent.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response to the client without blocking on downstream event processing.
behaviors.justification: A unit test with instant mocks would always show a fast response, even if the code accidentally awaits processing before sending the response. The real failure (response is delayed by processing) only surfaces when processing has real latency. A mock processor that completes instantly masks bugs in code structure (e.g., accidentally placed await, missing background task scheduler, or synchronous processing loop before response).
behaviors.test_description: POST a valid webhook request. After enqueue, start an asynchronous processing task that takes ≥2 seconds (e.g., mock I/O delay). Measure the wall-clock time from request arrival to HTTP 200 response. Verify: response arrives in <500ms (or similar latency threshold) while processing task is still running. Confirm processing eventually completes asynchronously after response is sent.

behaviors.description: The HTTP response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: A unit test with instant mocks would always pass the latency check. Real failure modes only appear under concurrent load with realistic queue and processing latencies: (1) queue contention delays response acknowledgment, (2) event processing for earlier requests blocks the response for new requests, (3) system resource contention (threads, memory) under load. These require multiple concurrent requests with real or simulated delays to expose.
behaviors.test_description: Send 10 concurrent HTTP requests with valid signatures. For each request, enqueue the event and then start an asynchronous processing task with a 5-10 second delay. Measure the wall-clock response time for each request from arrival to HTTP 200. Verify: all 10 responses arrive within 3 seconds. Run test multiple times and confirm p99 latency remains <3 seconds under concurrent load.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: The deduplication state (which event IDs have been seen) must be persistent across requests—either in a database, Redis cache, or similar durable store. A unit test with an in-memory mock would always deduplicate correctly, but would not catch failures in the real persistent store: (1) keys not being set/retrieved correctly from the database, (2) TTL or eviction rules causing false negatives, (3) race conditions in concurrent writes to the persistent store. Integration testing with a real or realistic persistent dedup store is required.
behaviors.test_description: Send two HTTP requests with identical event payload and event ID. Verify that the event ID appears in the persistent queue exactly once, not twice. Confirm the deduplication check against the persistent dedup store (e.g., by querying the database or cache directly) shows both requests were processed but only one enqueue succeeded.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 Service Unavailable with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: The error handling logic (catch queue-full exception, return 503 with Retry-After) is deterministic and testable without real infrastructure. A mock queue that throws a 'queue full' error will trigger the same error path as a real queue. The test is verifying the handler's error handling code, not the queue's capacity behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the queue's enqueue() method to throw an error indicating 'queue is full' or 'capacity exceeded'. POST a valid webhook request. Verify: (1) HTTP response status is 503, (2) Retry-After header is present and contains a valid integer (seconds), (3) the response body contains an appropriate error message.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This test is verifying the queue's own implementation, not just the handler's behavior. A naive mock might always allow enqueues or always reject them without actually tracking capacity. The real failure (queue silently accepts events beyond capacity, causing data loss or undefined behavior) only surfaces with a real or realistic queue implementation that tracks and enforces the limit.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a persistent queue with capacity limit of N (e.g., 5). Enqueue N events successfully. Verify all N are in the queue. Attempt to enqueue the (N+1)-th event. Verify: the enqueue operation fails (throws exception or returns error) and does not add the event to the queue. Confirm the queue size remains exactly N.
